summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's 2018 MacBook Pro refresh, noting it's squarely aimed at professional users with 6-core processors, up to 32GB RAM, and 4TB SSD options. He highlights that only Touch Bar models received updates, leaving consumer-tier machines unchanged. The bulk of his analysis focuses on the keyboard question — whether Apple's 'third-generation' butterfly keyboard also addresses the well-documented reliability problems, not just noise. He argues that Apple would say exactly the same thing regardless of whether they fixed reliability, because admitting the problem would be both a marketing and legal liability. He finds cautious optimism in the 'third-generation' branding, suggesting it implies more than a minor tweak.
key_points: Only high-end Touch Bar MacBook Pros were updated, with features like 32GB RAM and 4TB SSD clearly targeting professional users
32GB RAM is exclusive to the 15-inch model due to its switch from LPDDR3 to DDR4, requiring a different power architecture
Apple frames the keyboard changes as noise reduction, but Gruber argues they would say this regardless of whether reliability was also addressed
The 'third-generation' keyboard branding is the strongest signal that substantive engineering changes were made beyond noise reduction
True Tone displays debut on the Mac for the first time with these models
money_quotes: Features like supporting 4 TB of SSD storage and 32 GB of RAM are not consumer features.
The big question on many people's minds is the keyboards: Do they resolve the reliability issues that have surfaced ever since Apple switched to butterfly mechanisms?
Marketing-wise, I don't think they would admit to a reliability problem in the existing butterfly keyboards, and legal-wise I don't think they should admit to it.
So whether they've attempted to address reliability problems along with the noise or not, I think they'd say the exact same thing today: only that they've made the keyboards quieter.
To me the reason for optimism is that they're calling these keyboards 'third-generation', not just a quieter version of the second-generation butterfly-switch keyboards.
key_insight: Apple's decision to brand the new keyboard as 'third-generation' rather than an iteration of the second-generation design is the most telling signal about whether they've addressed reliability — because their legal and marketing positions guarantee they'd say exactly the same thing either way.
